## [00:00 - 03:04]

In this lesson, I'm going to show you advanced strategies on how to identify influences that align with your brand and convert to more sales. The problem these days is that anyone can be an influencer. Anyone can grow a large and engaged audience, but will just anyone convert for your brand? This is where a lot of brands who try influencer marketing say it's only provided brand awareness but doesn't work to boost sales. If you focus on influencers with reach, you'll get brand exposure. But if you focus on influencers with real influence, this is where they move the needle on your brand's behalf. Let's take a step back for a second and define what an influencer actually is. I would define an influencer as someone who doesn't only have their audience's attention but also has their trust and can leverage this pre-owned trust on your brand's behalf to influence their audience's buying decisions. And the keyword here is influence, the amount of influence that an influencer has over their audience. So what we're looking for here is the influential. If an influencer isn't aligned with your brand and can't inspire action in their audience, then they're simply a content creator with a following. As you can imagine, this is hugely important as every successful influencer campaign starts with selecting the right influencers and the results of your influencer marketing campaign will depend significantly upon your influencer selection. So we want to find and collaborate with the right influencers who can speak to your target audience on your brand's behalf. So before we can find the right influencers for your brand, you first need to understand who your target audience or ideal customer is. Get back to your customer personas here and analyze whether the influencer shares similar interests to your brand and your consumers. These are going to help inform the kinds of influencers that we're going to engage on your brand's behalf. If you haven't done a lot of work on customer personas, I've dropped a link in the resources section for this lesson. But what exactly constitutes the right influencer for your brand? Instead of simply looking at followers, a better equation for brands to consider is what I call the three Rs formula. You might recall this from start and scale. I use the three Rs formula to calculate influence, which is reach by relevancy by relationship equals influence. In start and scale, we only covered this on a surface level. But in this lesson, I'm going to dig much deeper into how I go about identifying influencers and the techniques that are working right now and are going to work long into the future. Our first R is reach, which is the easiest to measure as it's a quantitative figure. Reach is the size of an influencer's following and I use four different tiers to measure this. First up, we have our nano-influencers, which have anywhere from 1,000 to 10,000 followers.

## [03:04 - 06:05]

However, I'd rarely collaborate with influencers below 2,000 or 3,000 followers, but some brands do. So then we have our micro-influencers, who have between 10,000 and 50,000 followers. Then we move on to our mid-tier influencers, who have anywhere from 50,000 to 500,000 followers. And then our macro-influencers are influencers with 500,000 followers plus. Sometimes a fifth tier is added for influencers with 1 million plus followers, who are sometimes referred to as mega-influencers or power-influencers, but let's just stick to calling those macro-influencers here. There's always a lot of debate in the community on the merits of micro versus macro-influencers around cost, scalability, authenticity, engagement and ROI, but truth be told, all of the tiers of influencers have an important part to play in your overall marketing efforts. In later lessons, we'll be covering different ways to collaborate with nano and micro-influencers versus mid-tier and macro-influencers, because this does differ significantly. But when we're looking at reach, a mistake that I see brands making again and again is looking at reach as a static figure. Like okay, on this day and this time, I'm looking at this influencer and they have 400,000 followers. What's far more important than looking at reach as a static figure is looking at reach as a moving average. It sounds pretty obvious, but an influencer with 400,000 followers that's growing by 10,000 followers a month is more powerful than an influencer that's losing 10,000 followers a month. But honestly, this metric is rarely looked at. So month on month follower growth is what we're looking for here. That's not to say that influencers that aren't growing quickly still aren't powerful, but high growth influencers are going to be preferable and offer a unique advantage. At the fifth, we were able to lock some mid-tier influencers into three month contracts at their current pricing rate. In fact, it was actually at a reduced rate because it was before their growth really exploded. One of these influencers was a male Instagram influencer from Hawaii right after he'd started dating his girlfriend from LA. He had a far smaller following and was a mid-tier influencer compared to hers which already spanned into the millions. We were able to lock him into a series of posts over the space of three months, which we paid for in advance and as it was a multi-post deal, we even got these at a reduced rate. Three months later, he'd grown by a few hundred thousand followers and we were still paying reduced rates on his previous rate. So identifying the next up-and-coming influencer can be a really powerful strategy and a tool here that I use is a free tool called Social Blade. So I'll put a link to that in the resources section. Now let's move on to our second R in this formula, relevancy, which is going to help us identify whether the influencer is a good fit for our brand and even further than this, where their influence with their audience really lies.

## [06:05 - 09:09]

So what we're looking for here is what I call not influencers, but influencers. These are people with credibility on the topic that they're speaking about. So we can even use my personal Instagram profile at Greta. If I'm asked to speak on behalf of a skincare brand, I'm not going to lie, nobody would really care. However, when I posted about calming blankets, a brand that I advise and linked it back to helping me reduce the stress as a founder and better sleep for improved productivity, the posted really well and I sold around $2,000 worth of product for the brand with my discount code. This is because I'm making the product relevant to my audience. This is authentic and real and it makes sense for me to be posting. If we can add credibility onto that, say I was a sleep therapist or I'd written a book or done a study on the topic, then I'd clearly have more authority to be able to speak on the topic than others. The topic I do have more authority speaking on is of course business. So the ideal brand for me to push would be products that I'm already using within my businesses and how they've improved results for us. So that's where my influence truly lies. So with the influentials, what we're looking for are people who are credible or have an expert status in a specific subject area. So these are industry or category influencers. Some good examples of these might be influencers who are also podcasters, founders, authors, media platform owners, work in PR, award winners, doctors, nutritionists, specialists, list goes on. And it's really going to depend on your specific industry or category. So if you're a nutritional beverage brand, for example, I'd go with someone like Medical Medium who can be pretty much credited with the rise of the popularity of salary juice or other food as medicine style doctors that have a huge online and offline following. People like Dr. Axe, Dr. Mark Hyman and Dr. Oz. These influences are going to be able to leverage their authority and credibility on your brand's behalf to build trust as they're built an audience around their knowledge and skill set, not just their personality. So it's really important to start researching who your industry or category influencers might be. The way that I see it, your macro influencers should be category influencers who influence your micro influencers who should be closer representations of your customer personas and have a similar audience to themselves. This is something that I love about macro influencers. They're a great way to influence micro influencers. If a smaller influencer sees an influencer posting on your product, it basically serves as an ad to them saying this brand does influencer marketing and then they'll reach out if they're interested in working with you too. So working with macro influencers is a great way to get a lot of influencer marketing requests. So as you can see, it's really important to take into account the influencers niche, including their main areas of expertise, interests, skills and hobbies that they're actively

## [09:09 - 12:09]

posting on. Really identify where their influence lies and see whether they would have an influence over your target audience and the narrative that they should craft to do so in a relevant way. The question that you want to be asking yourself here is does this influencer have credibility or an expert status in a specific subject area? Have they built a brand around their personality, interests, hobbies or skills? Ask yourself who could educate your audience and add authority and credibility on your brand's behalf. And finally, the third R in this formula, relationship, refers to the relationship that the influencer has with their audience. This is their form of brand affinity, which is directly related to their credibility, authenticity and engagement with their audience. One of the best indicators of relationship is an influencer's engagement, especially comments. Relationships are built through two-way communication, so influencers who are responsive to their audiences will have a stronger relationship with their audience. The very, very best influencers have an almost cult-like following that hang on their every word and both actively seek out and take their recommendations. In terms of engagement, it's also important to look at the amount of engagement that the influencer receives on both their normal or organic posts, versus their sponsored posts, as engagement rates can sometimes drop significantly when influencers post-sponsored content. This might indicate a lack of authenticity in their branded content, or maybe even a weaker relationship with their audience. So their engagement is a useful indicator of the strength of their relationship with their audience. But the number one thing that we've found in all of the influencers that have converted the best across my brands, the brands that I advise and our clients at Hey Influencers, is the development of a relationship across multiple touchpoints and platforms, mainly via video and audio content, so YouTubers, people on TV and podcasters. At the fifth, we collaborated with a YouTube influencer who we paid $1,000 for a product review in one of her YouTube videos. I think she's around $15,000 a post now because her audience has grown so significantly. But for this $1,000 investment, we received $23,000 in attributed sales in return within the space of a couple of weeks. That's a 2,200% ROI, and the best part is that YouTube is an evergreen platform, so this post still converts to this day. When it comes to influencers who are TV personalities or have recently been on a TV show, for example, a reality TV series, a friend's brand who engaged a reality TV star, one of the bachelorettes in Australia, was able to achieve $20,000 in attributed sales from a single $500 sponsored Instagram post. And in terms of podcasters, a brand I advise, who is also a client of Hayes, received nearly $4,000 in attributed sales of one Instagram story. And it wasn't even sponsored.

## [12:09 - 13:29]

She'd actually bought the product, and our team picked it up and sent her a discount code for her followers in return for refunding her order. When we saw how well she converted for the client, we locked in a placement for her podcast, which cost $12,000, but we made $21,000 in attributed sales, not to mention we saw a big uplift in website traffic, which we were able to retarget to and convert later. So it's safe to say that these influencers have real influence that's developed through a deeper relationship that they've been able to establish with their audience. This is because video and even audio influencers are able to develop a more human connection with their audience because their audience feels like they know them, which helps scale trust that much further. So that was a deep dive into the three Rs. Reach, relevancy and relationship that help us identify influencers with real influence. As an action item for this lesson, I want you to make a list of influencers that would make sense for your brand under each of the three Rs.
